steps.prompt: A goalkeeper's view of the goal

graphic.name: goal-angle
graphic.caption: From anywhere on the circle, the goal opens up the same angle in a player's view. The center sees a wider angle than the players on the rim.

legend.label: central angle (corner at the center O)

steps.body:
Picture the goal posts as two fixed points, A and B, on a circle. Wherever a player stands ON the circle, the [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB is how wide the goal looks to them.
A [central angle](teal) has its corner at the center O. An [inscribed angle](red) has its corner ON the circle, at a point C.
Both are built from the same two points, A and B — this lesson finds how they're linked.

targets.label: Central angle
targets.hint: The central angle's corner is at the center, O.

targets.label: Inscribed angle
targets.hint: The inscribed angle's corner is on the circle, at C.

steps.doneNote: Both found — the central angle's corner is at O, the inscribed angle's corner is on the circle at C.

steps.subtitle: Keep A and B fixed and slide C along the circle. Watch closely: does the [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB change, or stay the same?

steps.prompt: Now meet O — compare the two angles
steps.subtitle: The center O makes the [central angle](teal) ∠AOB. Drag any point and compare it with the [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB. Which is bigger, and by how much?

steps.prompt: When AB is a diameter, what's true?
steps.subtitle: Now A and B are the ends of a diameter, so the [central angle](teal) ∠AOB is a straight 180°. C can be anywhere on the circle. Select everything that must be true.

options.label: The [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB is 90° — a right angle
Triangle ACB has its right angle at C
∠ACB stays 90° wherever C sits on the arc
∠ACB is 180°, the same as the central angle

feedback.correct: Yes — half of the 180° central angle is 90°, so ∠ACB is always a right angle. This is Thales' theorem.
feedback.incorrect: The inscribed angle is HALF the central angle. Half of 180° is 90°, no matter where C sits.

steps.doneNote: That's Thales' theorem: an angle drawn on a diameter is always a right angle (90°).

steps.body:
An [inscribed angle](red) is always HALF the [central angle](teal) that opens onto the same arc.
When ∠AOB is 120°, ∠ACB is 60°. Flip it around: the central angle is double the inscribed angle.
Remember the corners: the inscribed angle's corner sits ON the circle, the central angle's corner sits at the center O.

blanks.prompt: OC splits the 60° inscribed angle ∠ACB into two equal base angles. Each is 60 ÷ 2 = ?
Isosceles triangle OAC has base angles of 30° each, so ∠AOC = 180 − 2 × 30 = ?
∠AOB = 120° and the inscribed ∠ACB = 60°. The central angle is 120 ÷ 60 = ? times the inscribed angle.

stages: Draw a radius from O out to A, B, and C — every radius of a circle is the same length.
Two equal radii make triangles OAC and OBC isosceles, and an isosceles triangle has equal base angles.
OC splits the 60° inscribed angle, so those base angles at A, B, and C are each 30°.
A triangle's angles add to 180°, so ∠AOC and ∠BOC are each 120°. The angles at O fill a full turn of 360°, leaving ∠AOB = 120°.
120° = 2 × 60° — the inscribed angle is half the central angle.

steps.subtitle: A student finds an inscribed angle from its central angle. One line is wrong — tap it.

lines.text: The central angle AOB is 80 degrees.
The inscribed angle ACB sits on the same arc AB.
An inscribed angle is double the central angle, so ACB = 2 x 80 = 160 degrees.

steps.explanation: It's the other way around: an [inscribed angle](red) is HALF the [central angle](teal), not double. So ∠ACB = 80° ÷ 2 = 40°. The central angle is the bigger one.
steps.hint: Which angle is bigger — the one at the center, or the one on the circle? The inscribed angle is the smaller, half-size one.
steps.doneNote: Right — inscribed is half, not double.

feedback.incorrect: The inscribed angle is half the central angle.
The central angle is double the inscribed angle.
The inscribed angle is half the central angle.

steps.subtitle: A and B sit at the ends of a diameter, so the [central angle](teal) ∠AOB is 180°. What is the [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB?

feedback.correct: A right angle! You just used Thales' theorem: when AB is a diameter, ∠ACB is always 90°.
feedback.incorrect: Use the rule — ∠ACB is half of ∠AOB. What is half of 180°?

steps.subtitle: An [inscribed angle](red) ∠ACB is 70°. What is the [central angle](teal) ∠AOB?

feedback.correct: Done. The central angle is double the inscribed angle: 2 × 70° = 140°.
feedback.incorrect: The central angle is twice the inscribed angle.
